segments.s096-g1: شبی is a temporal adverbial placed at the start meaning “one night.” زال is the subject (proper name). بنشست is the simple past (3rd singular) verb ‘sat down’ with the old prefixed form بنشست. هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase (‘at the time of sleep/bedtime’) that further circumscribes when he sat.
segments.s096-g2: سخن گفت is a light-verb construction where سخن (‘words/speech’) is the object and گفت functions as ‘said/spoke’ (past). بسیار here is an adverbial meaning ‘much’/‘at length.’ ز is the poetic preposition ‘about/from’ introducing the person spoken of, and افراسیاب is the name that is the complement of ز.
segments.s096-g3: هم is an additive particle ‘also/too.’ از here means ‘of/regarding’ and introduces the following noun phrase. رزم‌زن نامداران خویش is a noun phrase: رزم‌زن ‘fighters/warriors’ qualified by نامداران ‘renowned ones,’ followed by the possessive خویش written as خوی + ش (see note on the enclitic), giving ‘his renowned warriors.’
segments.s096-g4: وزان is a fused sequence (و + ز آن) meaning roughly ‘and of those/and from that,’ introducing another list. پهلوانان is the plural ‘champions,’ و is the coordinating ‘and,’ یاران ‘companions,’ and again خوی + ش form the possessive ‘his (own).’ The phrase continues the list of people he mentioned.
segments.s096-g5: همی‌گفت is the habitual/imperfective past ‘kept saying’ (همی- marks continual or repeated action). هرچند is the subordinating conjunction ‘although.’ کز is the poetic fused form of که + از meaning ‘that/from which’ or simply ‘that’ here introducing the noun phrase پهلوان ‘(the) hero’ as the source/agent.
segments.s096-g6: بود is the past copula ‘was.’ بخت is the subject ‘fortune/fate’ and is described by the adjective بیدار ‘awake/alert.’ و links the second adjective روشن‌روان (‘bright‑souled’ or ‘cheerful-minded’). The clause thus predicates qualities of the hero’s fate/spirit: ‘his fate was alert and his soul bright.’
segments.s096-g7: بباید is a modal/existential ‘it is necessary/there ought to be.’ یکی is an indefinite ‘one/a certain.’ شاه خسرو‌نژاد is the noun phrase ‘a king, Khosrow‑nezhad’ (Khosrow‑nēzhād = descendant/line of Khosrow). The whole segment presents an expected/existing figure.
segments.s096-g8: که is the relative pronoun ‘who/that.’ دارد is present ‘has’ used idiomatically as ‘keeps/has in mind’ (i.e., remembers). گذشته modifies سخن‌ها as ‘past’ (گذشته سخن‌ها = ‘tales/speeches of the past’). به‌یاد is an adverbial/idiomatic phrase ‘in memory; remembering,’ so دارد ... به‌یاد literally ‘has past words in (his) memory.’
segments.s096-g89: ببد is an archaic past/copular verb here meaning 'became' (verb-first, a poetic word order). بخت ایرانیان is the logical subject 'the fortune/fate of the Iranians' (بخت = fortune, followed by the genitive Iranian plural ایرانیان). کندرو is a predicate nominative meaning 'incense' (a poetic metaphor: their fortune is described as fragrant/incense). The whole clause therefore reads verb → subject → predicate, a common inverted order in epic Persian.
segments.s096-g90: شد is a simple past/preterite meaning 'happened/rose' (here rendered 'arose/occurred'). آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to the person just named; دادگستر جهاندار is a two-part epithet in apposition — دادگستر 'dispenser of justice' and جهاندار 'sovereign/world‑holder'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'therefrom' used adverbially, and و here functions as an adverbial/continuative 'thereupon/then' linking this action to the previous clause. Grammatically the clause says that, thereupon, that just sovereign came/appeared.
segments.s096-g17: This is a poetic prepositional phrase + verb: ز + noun phrase ('ز تخم فریدون') = 'from the seed/lineage of Fereydun.' The verb بجستند is past, 3rd person plural, here meaning 'sprang/rose up' (an intransitive perfective); چند after the verb gives the quantity 'several / a few.' Word order is inverted compared with colloquial Persian because of the poetic style.
segments.s096-g18: یکی is an indefinite numeral ('one' / 'one of them') functioning as subject/appositive; the copula is omitted (typical in epic style), so 'یکی شاه' = 'one (of them) a king.' زیبای تخت بلند is a compact epithet: زیبای (adjective 'beautiful') qualifies the king, and تخت بلند ('the high/lofty throne') is a qualifying noun phrase that together forms an attributive phrase. The adjectives follow the noun they modify in this poetic nominal phrase and the relation is appositional.
segments.s096-g19: ندیدند is the past tense negative of 'see' (3rd pl.) used here idiomatically as 'they saw none' / 'they found none.' جز introduces the sole exception 'except.' پور طهماسپ is a name phrase 'the son (پور) Tahmasp.' ز here is the archaic/clitic preposition 'زو' (= از + او) meaning 'of him / his' (i.e., 'his son'); the last listed token (و) in the data appears to be part of that clitic and does not function as the conjunction 'and' here.
segments.s096-g20: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' زور کیان داشت is literal 'he had the strength/might of the Kayan (kings)' — زور = 'strength' as direct object of داشت ('had'). The conjunction و links a second predication, but the verb is omitted the second time (ellipsis is normal): فرهنگ گو is a compound adjective made of فرهنگ ('culture') + گو ('-speaking' / '-saying') meaning 'cultured/eloquent.' Grammatically, this clause attributes two qualities to Tahmasp: possession of royal strength and cultured speech.
segments.s096-g21: بشد is a past/copulative-like verb here ('it became' / 'there was / was made') in 3rd person sing.; it introduces a list of offices or persons who are now in place. The following coordinated nouns (قارن و موبد و مرزبان) are predicate nominatives listing the appointed roles (Qaren, the mōbed, the marzban).
segments.s096-g22: سپاهی is a collective noun meaning 'soldier(s) / a military contingent' here; the preposition ز ('from') marks origin or source: 'from Bamin and from the mace-bearer (گرزبان).' The two z‑phrases are coordinated by و. This segment lists additional military persons or contingents associated with the names/titles given.
segments.s096-g23: یکی here functions as 'one / someone' (indefinite person) and is the subject of the verb بردند (past, 3rd pl. of بردن = 'they brought'). مژده بردند = 'they brought good news.' نزدیک زو is a locative/goal phrase 'near him' where زو is the archaic clitic (= از/به + او) meaning 'to/near him'; in classical epic diction such clitics are common. The phrase has an omitted explicit agent in modern style but is grammatical in epic Persian.
segments.s096-g24: که introduces reported content 'that ...' تاج فریدون is a genitive phrase 'the crown of Fereydun.' به تو is the indirect object 'to you.' گشت is the past of گشتن 'became / came to be' (here 'came to/was bestowed upon'); نو 'new' functions adverbially/epithetically (poetic order) — 'became new to you' = 'has now come to you / has newly been yours.' The clause is a reported statement about a transfer of sovereignty.
segments.s096-g41: This is a negative, somewhat poetic clause. نیامد is the past negative 'did not come'; همی is an adverb indicating habitual or continuous aspect ('kept/continually'); ز آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'from the sky' (ز is the poetic 'from'); and هیچ نم (lit. 'any/moisture') is the quantified object meaning 'not even a drop of moisture'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: verb + aspect + source + quantified noun.
segments.s096-g42: همی before the verb makes the action habitual/persistent. برکشیدند is a past plural verb ('they raised/drew' in the dictionary sense) here used idiomatically to mean repeatedly obtaining or producing; نان is the direct object 'bread', and با درم is an instrumental/means phrase 'with dirhams' (i.e., by paying). The clause thus describes a recurring action: 'they would obtain bread by means of small coins.'
segments.s096-g43: دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies' (cardinal before noun). بران گونه is a frozen/poetic adverbial phrase meaning 'in that way' or 'thus' (بران = 'on/thereof/that', گونه = 'manner, way'). تا هشت ماه is a duration phrase 'for up to eight months' (تا marking the extent). The whole string gives who and for how long and in what manner.
segments.s096-g44: به روی اندر آورده روی سپاه is a compact, slightly archaic phrasing meaning 'they turned/ brought their faces toward the host' — i.e. 'they faced the army.' به (here 'towards/against') + روی ('face') with اندر آورده ('having brought/turned in') makes a verbal phrase; the final روی سپاه 'the face of the host' indicates what they faced. In prose this is rendered as 'they faced the army.'
segments.s096-g45: نکردند is the past negative plural 'they did not do/engage in.' یک‌روز here functions adverbially 'in a single day' or 'one day (once)'. جنگی گران is a noun phrase 'a heavy/serious battle' (جنگی formed from جنگ, with گران 'heavy/serious' as qualifier). The clause says no decisive/serious battle was fought in any single day.
segments.s096-g46: This clause is negative/elliptical. نه negates the following predicate: 'نه روز یلان بود' = 'it was not a day of heroes/champions.' The coordination 'و رزم سران' ('and the combat/battle of the chiefs') is elliptical: the verb (بود/was or نبود) is understood to extend to it, so the sense is 'nor was there a day for the champions, nor a battle of the leaders.' The clause uses the past copula بود and coordinates two related predications.
segments.s096-g47: ز تنگی is a causal phrase 'because of the scarcity/shortage' (ز = 'from/because of'). چنان شد که is the common resultive formula 'it became such that...' introducing the result clause; چاره نماند is 'no remedy/solution remained' with نماند (negated remain) as the final predicate. Overall: 'Because of the shortage it came to pass that there was no remedy left.'
segments.s096-g48: سپه را marks the army as the object/topic (را is the definite object marker). همی again gives habitual/continuous aspect. پود is literally 'weft/fabric'; here it is used metonymically/elliptically to mean 'patching/using up cloth to mend the army' (classical Persian often omits a light verb so a noun like 'پود' implies 'they made/used weft'). و تاره نماند 'and nothing fresh/new remained' — تاره 'new/fresh' qualified by نماند 'did not remain.' The effect: they were constantly patching the army's garments and no new supplies remained.
segments.s096-g33: This is a verbless descriptive clause with the past copula 'بود' placed between adjective and temporal phrase; the normal order would be 'مرد کهن بود بر سال هشتاد' meaning 'the man was old, eighty years (old)'. 'بر سال هشتاد' is a poetic expression meaning 'about/over eighty years (of age)'.
segments.s096-g34: 'به‌داد و به‌خوبی' are instrumental/advantageous phrases meaning 'with justice and with goodness' (the repeated 'به' makes both prepositional), followed by the verb 'تازه‌کرد' (past) 'renewed/made anew' governing 'جهان' as its object. The sentence thus states the manner (by justice and goodness) in which he 'renewed' the world.
segments.s096-g35: 'سپه را' uses the object/topic marker 'را' to mark 'the host/army' as the thing affected; 'ز' (poetic) = 'from' introduces the source/content 'کار بدی' ('evil deed(s)'). The compound 'باز داشت' (باز + داشت) functions as a past-tense verbal unit 'held back / restrained'.
segments.s096-g36: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason/explanation. 'با پاک یزدان' = 'with the pure God' (با = with; پاک modifies یزدان). 'یکی راز داشت' literally 'he had one secret' — 'یکی' emphasizes singularity ('a single/one' secret) and 'داشت' is the past-tense 'had'. Together this says he shared/possessed a private secret with God.
segments.s096-g37: 'گرفتن' and 'بستن' are infinitival/verbal nouns here meaning 'to capture' and 'to bind/secure'; the construction expresses incapacity/negative possibility ('could not'). 'نیارست' is an archaic/poetic negative/resultive auxiliary meaning 'it was not possible' or 'could not'; 'کسی' is the indefinite object 'anyone' governed by these verbs.
segments.s096-g38: 'وزان پس' = 'and from that time/thereafter'; 'ندیدند' is past negative 'they did not see'; 'کشتن' is a verbal noun 'killing' used as the object seen; 'بسی' is an adverb/quantifier meaning 'much/many' (classical usage). The clause says that thereafter they did not witness much killing.
segments.s096-g39: 'همان بد' = 'that same misfortune/evil'; 'که' introduces the content clause explaining it. 'تنگی بد' is a noun phrase 'severe scarcity' or 'narrowness of means' (تنگی = shortage, بد intensifies it), and 'اندر جهان' (اندر = in) places this hardship 'in the world'.
segments.s096-g40: 'شده' is the perfect/past participle 'has become' and 'خشک' is the adjective 'dry'; the phrase 'خاک و گیا را دهان' is a classical/genitive-like construction meaning 'the mouths of the earth and the plants' (را marks the possessed phrase), so the whole says 'the mouths of earth and vegetation had become dry.'
segments.s096-g73: سوی + place is a simple directional phrase: سوی زابلستان = 'toward Zabulistan'. بشد here is a past/resultative use meaning 'he/it went / it happened' (classical passive/preterite sense) and follows the directional phrase; زال is the subject (the hero Zal) and زر is an object or adverbial 'with gold' placed after the subject in poetic word order.
segments.s096-g74: This clause uses گرفتند (3rd person plural past) 'they took/seized'. جهانی functions adjectivally/nominally here as 'worldly / great (amount of) [gold]' modifying what was taken. هر یک به بر = 'each (one) on (his) arm' — به + بر is idiomatic poetic ordering meaning 'upon the arm'; هر یک is the distributive 'each'.
segments.s096-g75: پر از = 'full of' (پر here = 'full'), followed by a list of causes: غلغل و رعد 'clamor and thunder'. شد is the copular/verb of becoming 'became/was filled' and کوهسار ('the mountain') is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic order (verb before subject).
segments.s096-g76: زمین شد = 'the earth became' (شد = 'became'); again پر از = 'full of' introduces the list رنگ و بوی و نگار. نگار here is used in the sense of ornament/painting/beauty rather than 'beloved', so the sequence names visual and aromatic adornments of the earth.
segments.s096-g77: چون here is a simile particle 'like/as', so جهان چون عروسی رسیده جوان = 'the world, like a bride who has arrived/come (to her wedding), [is] young/young-looking'. رسیده is a past participle 'arrived/ripe' modifying عروسی; جوان is an adjectival predicate describing the resulting state.
segments.s096-g78: This continues the predicate: پر از = 'full of' followed by a series of items (چشمه 'springs', باغ 'gardens', آب روان 'flowing water'). روان here is adjectival 'flowing', modifying آب.
segments.s096-g79: چو is temporal 'when'; مردم is the subject 'people'. بدارد (3rd person singular here with a generic/impersonal reading) means 'possesses / takes on' and governs the object نهاد پلنگ 'the disposition/nature of a panther' — so the clause means 'when people take on a panther's nature'.
segments.s096-g80: بگردد is a verb meaning 'turn / become' (an intransitive/cohortative sense here: 'will turn/become'), زمانه is used as 'the times/fortune'. بر او = 'upon him'. تار و تنگ is a paired adjectival phrase meaning 'tight/narrow/constricting' (تار used metaphorically here), describing how the times act on him.
segments.s096-g57: سر نامداران (literally 'the heads of the famous') is the subject phrase; تهی شد is a past tense/resultative 'became empty' used here with a passive/figurative force (i.e. heads were taken). ز introduces the cause ('because of/from') and جنگ names the cause (war/battle). Note the poetic inversion and ellipsis common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s096-g58: ز تنگی is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of the hardship/straits'; نبد is an archaic negative/existential ('was not/did not exist') placed before روزگار درنگ, so the clause reads 'there was no pause in the course of time.' درنگ is the noun 'pause/delay'.
segments.s096-g59: بر آن is 'upon that / concerning that'; برنهادند is a past-tense verb 'they raised/put forward' (بر + نهادند), here meaning 'they began/spoke' or 'took up (the matter)'; هر دو سخن indicates 'both words / both (of them) spoke' — i.e. both spoke the same speech or both addressed it.
segments.s096-g60: که introduces the reported clause 'that ...'; در دل ندارند is literal 'they do not have in heart' (present-tense plural verb ندارند used to report their statement); کین کهن 'the old/ancient grudge' is the object phrase placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s096-g61: ببخشند here is a third-person plural verbal form meaning 'they give/bestow' (used as a hortative/subjunctive or narrative present after deliberation); گیتی is the object 'the world/realm'; به رسم و به داد is an adverbial phrase 'in accordance with custom and with justice' specifying the manner of bestowal.
segments.s096-g62: ز کار گذشته is a genitive/causal phrase 'from/of past deeds'; نیارند یاد is a compound idea 'they do not bring to mind / they do not recall' — here نیارند is a verbal negation in the plural and یاد is the noun 'memory/remembrance'. (Note: the provided lexical senses for نیارند do not match the verbal meaning; context shows it functions as 'do not recall'.)
segments.s096-g63: ز دریای gives the source 'from the sea'; پیکند is a classical past/plural verbal form 'they sent (messengers)' or 'they dispatched'; تا مرز تور is a destination phrase 'as far as the border of Tur' with تا indicating extent ('as far as'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s096-g64: ازان بخش گیتی 'from that part/region of the world' — ازان = 'from that', بخش = 'portion/district', گیتی = 'world'; ز نزدیک و دور is a parallel prepositional phrase 'from near and far' (ز = from), listing proximity and distance.
segments.s096-g81: Subject and object order: 'مهان را' (guests + object marker) marks 'the guests' as the direct object, followed by 'همه انجمن کرد' where 'همه' (all/everyone) modifies 'انجمن' (assembly) and 'کرد' is the past-tense verb 'made/caused' — together: 'made all the guests into an assembly' or 'gathered all the guests into an assembly'. The final short elements 'ز و' are the poetic contraction of 'از او' (from/because of him) written as separate particles here and provide a causal/agentive link to the preceding context.
segments.s096-g82: 'به دادار بر آفرین خواند نو' is a prepositional/verb phrase: 'به دادار' = 'to/for the Creator' (به + دادار), 'بر آفرین خواند' literally 'uttered/acclaimed (آفرین) upon/for him' with خواند as a past-tense verb meaning 'sang/uttered/called out', and 'نو' is an adverb/adjective 'new/fresh' modifying the acclamation. Word order is somewhat poetic (preposition + object + phrase + verb + modifier) but functionally: he voiced a renewed acclamation to the Creator.
segments.s096-g83: 'فراخی که آمد ز تنگی پدید' is a relative clause: 'فراخی' (spaciousness/abundance) is the head noun, 'که آمد' ('that came') is the relative clause marker + past verb, and 'ز تنگی پدید' ('padeed' = appeared/emerged from narrowness) uses the poetic 'ز' = 'from' before 'تنگی' (narrowness) and 'پدید' (appear) postposed for emphasis — literally: 'a spaciousness that came into being from narrowness.'
segments.s096-g84: This main clause has the Creator as subject: 'جهان‌آفرین' (the world‑creator) + past verb 'داشت' (had/possessed) + 'آن را' (that [spaciousness] as definite object) + 'کلید' (key). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object with the object marked by 'را'; meaning: 'the Creator had the key to it.'
segments.s096-g85: 'به هر سو' is a locative phrase 'to/at every direction/side', 'یکی جشن‌گه' = 'a single/a certain festive-place', and 'ساختند' is the past plural verb 'they built/made'. The order is prepositional phrase + object + verb = 'they built a festival-place on every side.'
segments.s096-g86: Literal structure: 'دل' (heart) + 'از' (from) introduces the things removed, 'کین و نفرین' (hatred and curse), and the verb 'بپرداختند' (past plural) here means they 'paid away / cast off / dispensed with' — idiomatically 'they rid their hearts of hatred and curse.' The verb is transitive and takes the whole preceding phrase as its object or complement.
segments.s096-g87: 'چنین تا برآمد برین سال پنج' is a temporal clause: 'چنین' = 'thus/so', 'تا' = 'until', and 'برآمد' = 'arose/came'. The phrase 'برین سال پنج' is an archaic/poetic temporal expression meaning 'this/fifth year (or these five years coming up)' — together: 'so it continued until the coming of the fifth year.'
segments.s096-g88: Negated existential clause: 'نبودند' is past plural negative 'there were not/was not', 'آگه کس' = 'anyone aware' (here in the scope of negation = 'no one aware'), and 'از درد و رنج' ('of/from pain and suffering') names what they were unaware of. The whole means 'no one was aware of pain and suffering.'
segments.s096-g49: سخن رفتشان is an idiomatic expression: سخن (speech/words) is the theme and رفتشان (رفت + ـشان) indicates that the words 'went from them' — i.e. they spoke. یک به‌یک is an adverbial phrase meaning 'one by one' (in turn). هم‌زبان here qualifies how they spoke: 'with the same tongue/words', so the whole segment means 'they spoke, one by one, in the same words.'
segments.s096-g50: که is the conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. از ماست is a verbless clause with از used causally ('because of/of us') plus the copula ست 'is' (here contracted classical form), so 'it is because of us'. بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon us' and بد آسمان is a nominal phrase 'the sky/heaven [is] bad/hostile' with بد as adjective modifying آسمان. Together: 'that it is because of us that heaven is hostile to us.'
segments.s096-g51: ز هر دو سپه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/because of both hosts' (هر دو = both), خاست is the classical past 'arose/began', and فریاد و غو are a pair of nouns 'shout/cry and uproar/tumult'. Grammatically: 'From both armies arose a cry and a tumult.'
segments.s096-g52: فرستاده آمد is straightforward: 'a messenger came'. به نزدیک + زو is an adverbial 'to/near him'; in older Persian زو is a contracted archaic form corresponding to از او/به او ('to him' / 'near him'). The word-list split (ز + و) reflects that contraction; syntactically the phrase means 'came near him.'
segments.s096-g53: که‌گر is an archaic conditional 'if' (introducing the subordinate idea). بهر ما means 'for us' or 'on our behalf'. زین سرای سپنج is a compact noun phrase 'زین سرای سپنج' — in the manuscript this cluster is somewhat archaic/obscure: زین is not used here as the demonstrative 'this' in classical diction and سرای would normally be 'house/place', while سپنج is an unusual noun. Grammatically the whole segment functions as the protasis: 'if for us from this [place/house] ...' (the precise lexical senses of زین / سرای / سپنج are archaic/uncertain in this line).
segments.s096-g54: نیامد به جز is a fixed negative-except construction: نیامد 'did not come' + به جز 'except' → 'nothing came except'. The list that follows (درد و اندوه و رنج) are coordinated nouns 'pain, sorrow and suffering.' So the clause reads 'nothing came except pain, sorrow and suffering.'
segments.s096-g55: بیا is a hortative/imperative 'come' used to invite action = 'let us'. تا here introduces a purposive/subsequent subjunctive ('so that' / 'in order that' → here functioning as 'let us [do X]'): بیا تا ببخشیم = 'come, let us forgive'. روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the face/surface of the earth' or simply 'on earth.'
segments.s096-g56: سراییم is the hortative/subjunctive/1st-person-plural of سراییدن (here 'to compose/sing'), yielding 'let us sing/compose.' یک با دگر is an idiom 'one with the other' (i.e. mutually/alternately). آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise' (not the verb 'to create') — so the phrase means 'let us sing together, each praising the other' or 'let us offer acclamation one to another.'
segments.s096-g65: روارو چنین تا به چین و ختن is a single adverbial/extent phrase. روارو and چنین together set the manner ('thus/so'); تا ... به ... marks the limit/extent 'as far as/to'; چین و ختن are place names ('China and Khotan'). Word order is demonstrative/adverbial (manner) + extent marker + destination. The whole phrase modifies the following predicate (they entrusted...).
segments.s096-g66: سپردند شاهی بران انجمن is a past action clause. سپردند is 3rd-person plural past 'they entrusted/handed over'; شاهی is the direct object 'kingship'; بران ('بر آن') is a locative/relational particle 'upon/over that'; انجمن is the noun 'assembly/realm' receiving the kingship. The verb-final order (object before particle/noun) is normal in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s096-g67: ز مرزی کجا مرز خرگاه بود literally 'from a frontier where the boundary was a donkey‑stall.' ز ('from') introduces the source; مرزی is 'a frontier/boundary' and کجا 'where'; the clause مرز خرگاه بود uses verbless relative order where مرز (boundary) + خرگاه (donkey‑stall/lowly place) + بود ('was') yields 'the boundary was a donkey‑stall.' The sentence uses poetic inversion but conveys location and lowly condition.
segments.s096-g68: ازو زال را دست کوتاه بود means 'from it/there Zāl had a short hand' i.e. little power. ازو ('from it/of it') sets the locus; زال را uses را as the topical/definite object marker marking Zāl as the patient/topic; دست کوتاه بود is an idiomatic verbless construction literally 'the hand was short' meaning 'he had little reach/authority.' بود is past copula 'was.'
segments.s096-g69: وزین روی ترکان نجویند راه is an adverbial causal phrase followed by a finite clause. وزین (an archaic/poetic form: وز + این) functions as 'because of this / for this reason' (here treated as an introductory particle), روی = 'face/condition' specifies 'this state', then ترکان is the subject 'the Turks', نجویند is the present/habitual verb 'they do not seek' (negation implicit in this context of 'not seeking' depends on the sense), and راه is the object 'way/path.' Subject–verb–object order is clear: 'the Turks do not seek the way.'
segments.s096-g70: چنین بخش کردند تخت و کلاه is a past-result clause. چنین 'thus/so' links to the foregoing; بخش (here) names a 'portion/gift/district' or more generally 'a share' and with کردند ('they made/did') yields 'they apportioned/distributed'; تخت و کلاه are the direct objects listed in coordination ('throne and helmet/crown'). The verb گذشت/کردند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they allocated.'
segments.s096-g71: سوی پارس لشکر برون راند ز (سوی پارس = 'toward Pars') is a directional prepositional phrase followed by the verb cluster. لشکر is the direct object/thing sent 'an army', برون 'out/forth' is an adverb modifying the verb, and راند is the past verb 'he sent/drove/expelled.' The following short particle ز begins the next clause ('from/of'), so the core here is 'he sent forth an army toward Pars.' Word order places the directional phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s096-g72: کهن بود لیکن جهان کرد نو is a compact two-part statement. کهن بود = 'he/it was old' (کهن + بود, past copula), لیکن = 'but/however' introduces the contrast, and جهان کرد نو = literally 'made the world new' where کرد is past 'made/did' and نو is the adjective 'new' following its object جهان. The clause thus contrasts age with a rejuvenating action: although old, he renewed the world.
segments.s096-g25: This is a coordinated noun phrase: 'سپهدار' (a military title) followed by the name/identifier 'دستان' (here functioning as a proper name), joined by 'و' and the quantified noun 'یکسر سپاه' = 'the entire army.' Word order is Title/Name + و + quantified noun.
segments.s096-g26: ت + را together form the object 'ترا' = 'you' (the old poetic orthography separates the pronoun and the object marker). 'خواستند' is third-person plural past 'they sought/desired/asked for.' 'ای سزاوار گاه' is a vocative phrase: 'O (ای) deserving/fit (سزاوار) of the moment/time (گاه),' addressing the addressee.
segments.s096-g27: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' 'بشنید' is past tense 'he heard.' 'زو' is poetic compression of ز + او = 'from him/of him.' 'گفته موبدان' is a genitive phrase 'the saying/words of the mobeds' (موبدان = Zoroastrian priests).
segments.s096-g28: همان گفته = 'the same statement/words.' The following nouns 'قارن و بخردان' name other speakers/groups who repeated the same statement; 'و' links them. Here 'قارن' functions as a name/agent and 'بخردان' means 'the wise (ones).' The clause is elliptical/poetic: 'the same was said by Qaran and the wise.'
segments.s096-g29: The verb 'بیامد' (he/it came) opens the line in classical order. 'به نزدیک ایران' is a locative phrase 'near/close to Iran' and 'سپاه' (army) is the (grammatical) subject placed after the locative for poetic effect: 'the army came near to Iran.'
segments.s096-g30: 'به سر بر نهاده' is a participial/phrased clause meaning 'having placed (بر نهاده) on the head (به سر).' 'کیانی کلاه' is a genitive compound 'the Kayanian hat' — i.e. the royal crown. Classical word order places the participle and its object after the locative; literal: 'the Kayanian crown placed upon the head.'
segments.s096-g31: This clause is organized in an older-Persian order where the compliment and object precede the verb and the subject comes last. به‌شاهی is a prepositional/nominal phrase meaning “for/as king”; بر او is a dative/benefactive phrase “to him”; آفرین is an interjection ‘praise/“bravo”’; خواند is the past tense verb ‘uttered/said/sang’ that applies to آفرین; and زال at the end is the subject performing the action (’Zal praised’).
segments.s096-g32: نشست is the simple past (3rd sg.) of نشستن used narratively (‘sat/took his seat’ or ‘reigned’). از بر تخت is an archaic/prepositional cluster: بر تخت = ‘upon the throne’, and the preceding از can mark the beginning/duration in this idiom (so the whole phrase means ‘took his seat upon the throne’). The token ز here is the poetic preposition/particle that fuses with the following pronoun (زو = ز + او) to indicate possession (‘his’); the tokenization provided splits them, so the listed و actually represents the pronoun element, not the conjunction. پنج سال is a duration phrase ‘five years’ modifying نشست.
segments.s096-g9: به‌کردار is a prepositional/adiuvantial phrase meaning 'in conduct' or 'in action' that sets the domain for the statement. کشتی‌ست is the copular clause (کشتی + ـست) = 'is a ship'; the verb comes attached as the old poetic copula. کار سپاه is the noun phrase 'the work/affair of the army' (کار governs سپاه), so literally 'In action, the army's affair is a ship.'
segments.s096-g10: همش باد is an optative wish: همش 'all of it' + باد here functions as an optative particle 'may it be' (classical/poetic use) so 'may it all be [so / wind].' The following و هم introduces a second coordinated wish 'and also ...'; بادبان تخت شاه is a genitive phrase 'the sail of the king's throne' (بادبان = sail, تخت شاه = the king's throne). The verb ('be'/'may be') is left implicit and understood from باد in the first clause.
segments.s096-g11: اگر داردی is the protasis 'if you/they have' using the older poetic 2nd‑person form داردی; the verb precedes the subjects in this word order. طوس و گستهم فر is the coordinated subject + object: 'Tus and Gostaham (possess) farr' where فر (farr) = royal/divine glory. (Note: گستهم here refers to the name Gostaham; the provided senses do not include the proper‑name sense, so it is treated as a name.)
segments.s096-g12: سپاه است is a verbless/existential statement with the copula 'است' = 'there is / is an army.' The coordination و گردان بسیار مر links another predicate: گردان بسیار = 'many units/troops' and مر is an archaic/dialectal object/pronoun meaning 'to me' or 'mine' (i.e. 'many battalions are mine'). Word order places the copula early and leaves 'مر' as the recipient/possessor marker.
segments.s096-g13: نزیبد is the negative/form of زیبیدن in poetic usage meaning 'it does not become / it is not fitting.' بر ایشان = 'upon them' (i.e. 'for them'); همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just'); تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne.' The clause states that wearing crown and throne is not fitting for those people.
segments.s096-g14: بباید is the impersonal/poetic necessity 'it must / there ought to be.' یکی شاه = 'one king' (یکی as numeral/indefinite 'one'); بیداربخت is a descriptive epithet 'ill‑fated' or 'unlucky' modifying شاه. The verb precedes the subject, a common poetic order: 'There must be one ill‑fated king.'
segments.s096-g15: که introduces the purpose/result clause 'that/so that.' باشد is the subjunctive/optative 'may be' or 'be (so)' and بدو = 'to him' (enclitic prepositional pronoun). فره ایزدی is a noun phrase 'divine farr/glory' (فره = farr, ایزدی = divine) — together 'that divine glory may be to him / upon him.'
segments.s096-g16: بتابد is again an optative/third‑person verb 'may it shine' (from تافتن/تابیدن). ز = 'from' (poetic old preposition). دیهیم here refers to native/folk/land ('his native land/people' or 'villagers') and او = 'his'/'him' (referring back to the king). بخردی (به خردی) means 'by/through wisdom' or 'by his prudence.' So the clause says 'may it shine from his homeland by his wisdom.'

sentences.s096-s1: One night Zāl sat down at bedtime and spoke at length about Afrasiab.
sentences.s096-s2: He also spoke of his famed warriors, and likewise of those champions and his companions.
sentences.s096-s3: He kept saying that, although the hero’s fate was wakeful and his spirit bright.
sentences.s096-s4: There ought to be a certain king, a descendant of Khosrow, who keeps the tales of the past in mind.
sentences.s096-s45: The fortune of the Iranians became incense; thereupon that dispenser of justice and sovereign arose.
sentences.s096-s9: From the seed of Fereydun sprang several; one was a king, beautiful, of the lofty throne.
sentences.s096-s10: They saw none except his son Tahmasp, who had the strength of the Kayan kings and was cultured/eloquent.
sentences.s096-s11: There became (were) Qaren and the mobed and the marzban, and a (contingent of) soldiers from Bamin and from the mace-bearer.
sentences.s096-s12: Someone brought tidings near him that the crown of Fereydun had newly come to you.
sentences.s096-s21: Not a single drop of moisture came from the sky; they kept getting bread with dirhams.
sentences.s096-s22: For eight months the two armies, in that fashion, faced one another.
sentences.s096-s23: They did not fight one single heavy battle in a day; there was no day of champions and no battle of the chiefs.
sentences.s096-s24: Because of the scarcity it became such that no remedy remained; they kept patching (the army's clothing), and nothing fresh remained.
sentences.s096-s17: The man was old — eighty years — and with justice and goodness he renewed the world.
sentences.s096-s18: He restrained the host from evil deeds, for he had one secret with the pure God.
sentences.s096-s19: They could not capture or bind anyone, and after that they did not see much killing.
sentences.s096-s20: Such was the ill — the world's hardship was so severe — that the mouths of earth and vegetation became dry.
sentences.s096-s37: Zal set out toward Zabulistan with gold; they seized worldly gold, each upon his arm.
sentences.s096-s38: The mountain grew full of clamor and thunder; the earth became full of color, scent, and ornament.
sentences.s096-s39: The world, like a newly arrived bride, became youthful — full of springs, gardens, and flowing water.
sentences.s096-s40: When people assume the nature of a panther, the times turn upon him tight and constricting.
sentences.s096-s29: The heads of the renowned were emptied by war; because of hardship there was no pause in the course of time.
sentences.s096-s30: On that they both spoke, declaring that they harbored no ancient grudge in their hearts.
sentences.s096-s31: They bestowed the realm according to custom and justice, and they did not recall past deeds.
sentences.s096-s32: From the sea they sent messengers as far as Tur's border, to that region of the world from near and far.
sentences.s096-s41: He gathered all the guests into an assembly, and for the Creator he uttered a fresh acclamation.
sentences.s096-s42: A spaciousness that came forth from narrowness appeared — the Creator held its key.
sentences.s096-s43: They built a place of celebration on every side, and they cast off hatred and curses from their hearts.
sentences.s096-s44: Thus, until the coming of the fifth year, no one was aware of pain and suffering.
sentences.s096-s25: They spoke, one by one, in the same words: that it is because of us that heaven is hostile toward us.
sentences.s096-s26: From both hosts arose cries and tumult; a messenger came close to him.
sentences.s096-s27: For if, for us, from this house nothing came except pain, sorrow and suffering.
sentences.s096-s28: Come, let us forgive; on the face of the earth let us sing together, each one praising the other.
sentences.s096-s33: Thus they extended (their authority) as far as China and Khotan, and they entrusted kingship over that assembly.
sentences.s096-s34: From a frontier whose boundary was but a donkey‑stall; Zāl had little power there.
sentences.s096-s35: Because of this, the Turks sought no path; thus they apportioned throne and crown.
sentences.s096-s36: Toward Pars he sent forth an army; though he was old, he made the world new.
sentences.s096-s13: The sepahdar Dastan and the whole army sought you, O worthy one of the hour.
sentences.s096-s14: When he heard from him the words of the mobeds, Qaran and the wise men said the same.
sentences.s096-s15: The army came near to Iran; the Kayanian crown was placed upon his head.
sentences.s096-s16: Zal acclaimed him as king; the king sat upon his throne for five years.
sentences.s096-s5: In action the army's work is a ship; may all be wind, and may the king's throne be its sail.
sentences.s096-s6: If Tus and Gostaham possess farr (divine glory), there is an army, and many battalions belong to me.
sentences.s096-s7: Crown and throne do not become them; there must be one ill‑fated king.
sentences.s096-s8: so that divine farr may be upon him; may it shine from his native land by his wisdom.
